coredata.dc:title: Superhydrophobic carbon nanodot-tube/MXene/microfiber coupling textile for highly reliable amphibious human motion monitoring
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Song, Haoyang
Liang, Yibo
Ma, Junchi
Cai, Yang
Wang, Yibo
Wang, Ximan
Zhang, Hongjia
Liu, Changsheng
Qing, Yongquan
coredata.dc:description: Superhydrophobic flexible strain sensors exhibit significant potential for detecting human behavior in amphibious environments. However, stable monitoring of amphibious human motion remains a notable challenge, as electronic devices exposed to wet environments, particularly corrosive liquids, can lose their effectiveness due to damage to conductive networks. Herein, we report the design of a superhydrophobic carbon nanodot-tube/MXene/microfiber coupled textile (SCNCT) by dip-coating a 2D conductive adhesive layer, followed by spray-coating a suspension of fluorinated 0D/1D conductive networks onto substrates. Such material retains excellent superhydrophobicity even after mechanical damages and soaking in 0.1 M strong acid/alkali and 3.5 wt% saline for 3 h. After enduring various tests, including ultrasonic vibrations, continuous solid impacts, extreme thermal conditions, and applications involving multiple limb deformations, the SCNCT still remains capable of stably outputting signals. Importantly, the SCNCT, equipped with a multi-dimensional interlocking structure, reliably monitors the full-range human behaviors across various conditions—wet environments, underwater, and even in simulated seawater. This work offers a simple and practical solution for developing highly reliable strain sensors with a response that is stable in extremely wet environments.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Flexible strain sensors
Superhydrophobic
Robustness
Interlocking structure
Amphibious environment

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensing properties and human monitoring capability
Stable sensing properties under amphibious environments

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Design strategy of SCNCT. (a) Fabrication process of SCNCT by dip-coating with MXene/chitosan and spray-coating of fluorinated carbon nanodot-tube suspension on hydrophilic textiles. (b) Ball-stick model of MXene combined with chitosan. Schematic illustration of (c) the multi-dimensional interlocking structure and (d) water droplets on micro/nano structure for SCNCT surface. (e) Sensing mechanism of SCNCT during strain. Wetting states of SCNCT surface under (e) water impact and (f) underwater conditions. (h) Photographs showing the anti-wettability of SCNCT surface with various common liquids closely related to daily life. Schematic illustration of SCNCT's application emitting response signals in (i) wet and (j) underwater environments.

Morphology and composition of textiles. SEM image and surface wettability of (a) hydrophlic textile, (b) MXene textile, and (c) SCNCT. (d) Elmental mappings of main elements C, F, O, N and Ti on the SCNCT. LSCM image of (e) hydrophlic textile and (f) SCNCT.

Surface composition. (a) FTIR spectra of hydrophlic textile, MXene textile, and SCNCT. XPS (b) survey spectrum, (c) high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s, and (d) Ti2p of MXene-chitosan. XPS (e) survey spectrum and (f) C1s for the fluorinated carbon nanodot tube.

Superhydrophobic robustness of SCNCT. SCNCT under physical effect: CA and SA during (a) 1000 stretching cycles, (b) 1000 impacts, (c) 200 peeling cycles, (d) 2 m abrasion, (e) suffering from different pH liquids, (f) 60 d of UV irradiation with wind, and (i) 3 h soaking in 0.1 M HCl, NaOH, and 3.5 wt% NaCl solutions. SCNCT's Surface morphology after (g) 1000 stretching cycles, (h) 1000 impacts, (j) 200 peeling cycles, (k) 2 m abrasion. Surface morphology of (l, n) hydrophilic MXene textile soaked and (m, o) SCNCT in 0.1 M HCl and 0.1 M NaOH solution for 3 h, respectively.

Stable sensing property of SCNCT. (a) Relative resistance change rate as a function of tensile strain. Gauge factors obtained from the sensing curve that is divided into two linear strain regions. (b) Sensing response and recovery time for a 2 mm strain applied at 400 mm/s. Relative resistance change rate under various conditions: (c) different tensile forces at small strains, (d) the same strain at varying frequencies, (e) different strains at a constant frequency, and (f) different bending angles. SCNCT's sensor signal stability after various treatments: (g) 2000 cycles of -20% strain, (h) 2 h of ultrasound exposure, (i) 1000 impacts with a 50 g weight, (j) heating at 70 °C, and (k) refrigeration at −20 °C.

SCNCT's monitoring of human behaviors. (a) Throat swallowing signals at different bending angles. (b) Heartbeat signals during various exercise states. Neck-worn material signals for (c) head lifting, (d) lowering, (e) tilting, and (f) rotation. Monitoring signals of different limb movements: (g) elbow bending, (h) palm opening/closing, (i) wrist bending, (j) finger bending, (k) calf muscles relaxing/tension, and (l) Knee bending.

Sensing stability and corrosion mechanism in wet environments. Sensing curves of droplets falling from (a, c) hydrophilic MXene textiles and (b, d) SCNCT surfaces without/with attached dust. Sensing cycle curves of (e) hydrophilic MXene textile and (g) SCNCT before and after condensation. (f) Schematic of the condensing device. Image and surface morphologies of hydrophilic MXene textile wetted by (h, i) liquid and (k, l) mud mixture. Schematic diagrams of (j) liquid and (m) muddy mixture corrosion on hydrophilic MXene textile.

Stable sensing properties of SCNCT underwater. Sensing signals of (a, h) hydrophilic MXene textile and (b, i) SCNCT in air, underwater or under saline, and return to the air, respectively. Sensing signal intensity of SCNCT with different bending angles under (c) underwater and (j) saline. Sensing signal of SCNCT slowly entering (d) water and (k) saline from the air. Sensing cycles curve of SCNCT (e) underwater and (l) in saline with 400 cycles. Mechanism affecting the sensing and monitoring of (f) the hydrophilic MXene textile and (g) SCNCT underwater and in saline.

head.ce:title: Superhydrophobic carbon nanodot-tube/MXene/microfiber coupling textile for highly reliable amphibious human motion monitoring

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Northeastern University, Shenyang, 110819, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Northeastern University, Shenyang 110819, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Anisotropy and Texture of Materials, Ministry of Education, Northeastern University, Shenyang, 110819, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Anisotropy and Texture of Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory for Anisotropy and Texture of Materials, Ministry of Education, Northeastern University, Shenyang 110819, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Textile and Clothing College, Liaodong University, Dandong, 11779, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Textile and Clothing College, Liaodong University, Dandong 11779, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Materials Science and Engineering, Northeastern University, Shenyang, 110819, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Superhydrophobic flexible strain sensors exhibit significant potential for detecting human behavior in amphibious environments. However, stable monitoring of amphibious human motion remains a notable challenge, as electronic devices exposed to wet environments, particularly corrosive liquids, can lose their effectiveness due to damage to conductive networks. Herein, we report the design of a superhydrophobic carbon nanodot-tube/MXene/microfiber coupled textile (SCNCT) by dip-coating a 2D conductive adhesive layer, followed by spray-coating a suspension of fluorinated 0D/1D conductive networks onto substrates. Such material retains excellent superhydrophobicity even after mechanical damages and soaking in 0.1 M strong acid/alkali and 3.5 wt% saline for 3 h. After enduring various tests, including ultrasonic vibrations, continuous solid impacts, extreme thermal conditions, and applications involving multiple limb deformations, the SCNCT still remains capable of stably outputting signals. Importantly, the SCNCT, equipped with a multi-dimensional interlocking structure, reliably monitors the full-range human behaviors across various conditions—wet environments, underwater, and even in simulated seawater. This work offers a simple and practical solution for developing highly reliable strain sensors with a response that is stable in extremely wet environments.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Flexible strain sensors, lightweight, pliable, and highly responsive, have attracted significant attention due to their ability to translate mechanical deformations into electrical signals [1–4]. This unique capability has led to their widespread applications in various fields such as healthcare [5,6], environmental monitoring [7], energy harvesting [8], industrial safety [9,10], and human-computer interaction [11]. By regulating microstructures, including porous, hollow, micro-cone, and interlocking architectures [12–15], and incorporating sensing components such as graphene, MXenes, carbon nanotubes (CNTs), metallic nanostructures, carbon black, and conductive polymers, researchers have synthesized a range of high-performance flexible sensors [16–19]. MXene, known for its unique 2D structure and electrochemical properties [20,21], has advanced sensor technology through innovations such as high-stretch nanogenerators [22] and dual conductive networks [23]. Its superior conductivity and mechanical strength boost both the durability and accuracy of sensors. In real-world applications, stability is crucial for the performance and response longevity of flexible strain sensors. Nevertheless, exposure to harsh environments, such as aquatic settings, acids, bases, and saline, can undermine their conductive networks, disrupt operations, and ultimately shorten their lifespan. For example, sensors often encounter water during sweating and diving; immersion can lead to short circuits, posing safety risks to users and causing the decomposition and oxidation of conductive materials, reducing performance [24,25].

Superhydrophobic surfaces, with a water contact angle (CA) exceeding 150° and a sliding angle (SA) below 10°, are ideal for flexible sensing materials to resist liquid interference [26–28]. These surfaces induce a Cassie-Baxter wetting state, preventing liquid contact and penetration, thereby ensuring stable sensing performance in humid or corrosive environments [29–31]. Typically, integrating micro/nanostructures and low-surface-energy materials into conductive substrates is a key strategy for endowing flexible sensors with superhydrophobic properties [32–34]. Sun et al. [35] reported a superhydrophobic strain sensor by decorating elastic fibers with a composite of CNTs/reduced graphene oxide and hydrophobic fumed SiO2, which demonstrates comprehensive monitoring of human dynamics and effective recognition of signals, including underwater motion. However, these superhydrophobic flexible sensors often degrade or suffer damage in extreme corrosive or adverse operational environments, leading to the collapse of their superhydrophobic structure and irreversible mechanical damage to the material surface, which disrupts their normal functionality [36–38].

To enhance the stability of superhydrophobic flexible sensors, insulating hydrophobic elastomers have been used for encapsulation, effectively isolating moisture and enabling underwater operation. Ma et al. [39] created an underwater strain sensing material by encapsulating an ionic gel layer within an elastomer. Nevertheless, encapsulation limits sensor sensitivity and external stimulus perception, rendering it applicable only for underwater strain monitoring. Furthermore, Qing et al. [40] fabricated a superhydrophobic electronic textile with robust mechanical properties by coupling a Ti3C2Tx conductive bottom layer with a hierarchical papillae-structured composite top layer, suitable for wet environment sensing. This material maintains its superhydrophobicity and high sensitivity in underwater environments, comparable to air, even after enduring mechanical abrasion, light exposure, and water immersion. Despite these advancements, achieving stable monitoring with superhydrophobic flexible sensors in harsh conditions—including physical interactions, exposure to strong acids/alkalis, high humidity, and underwater environments—continues to be challenging.

To address these challenges, we designed and fabricated a superhydrophobic textile (SCNCT), which combines carbon nanodot-tubes, MXene, and microfibers. Such material features a multi-dimensional structure where points, lines, and surfaces are interlocked. Remarkably, the SCNCT retains excellent superhydrophobicity even under mechanical damage, such as strain stretching and weight impact, as well as after immersion in 0.1 M strong acid/alkali and 3.5 wt% saline solution for 3 h. In terms of sensing performance, SCNCT shows a wide monitoring range, rapid response time, and precise sensitivity, enabling reliable monitoring of human strain behaviors. Importantly, its robust interlocked structure endows SCNCT accurate monitoring and stable operation in wet, underwater, and saline environments. Our research paves the way for the superhydrophobic flexible strain sensors tailored for complex amphibious environments.

To achieve the SCNCT for highly reliable monitoring human motion in amphibious environments, we adopted a strategy that intertwines multi-dimensional structures—specifically, points, lines, and planes. This approach integrates superhydrophobic and conductive structures onto flexible textiles, enhancing durability and corrosion resistance in both wet and underwater conditions. The design of SCNCT follows three principles: (i) flexible and elastic substrates for strain response and stability; (ii) conductive networks with strong conductivity and excellent adhesion; and (iii) a robust structure featuring a superhydrophobic surface with low surface energy, all while maintaining conductivity.

To meet the first principle, we selected a commercial elastomeric spandex textile composed of a blend of spandex and polyester due to its high strength, elasticity, and abrasion resistance. Compared to silicone rubber substrates, the textile's exceptional breathability, reactive surface groups, and outstanding elasticity are adequate for meeting wearable strain sensing. Accordance with the secondary principle, layered MXene was chosen characterized by its high specific surface area, surface activity, superior mechanical properties, and conductivity. This layered structure effectively adheres to the hydrophilic textile, encapsulating the fiber framework to form a contiguous conductive network (Fig. 1 a). Moreover, the incorporation of chitosan enhances the bonding strength between MXene and the textile via chemical bonding and physical adsorption, significantly improving MXene adhesion and the integrity of the conductive network (Fig. 1b).

Following the third principle, the selected hydrophilic textile underwent repeated immersion in an MXene-chitosan solution, followed by spraying with fluorinated carbon nanodot-tubes. This process led to the substitution of hydrophilic hydroxyl groups in MWCNTs and carbon black with fluorinated groups, yielding a material with low surface energy (Fig. 1c). In a semi-dry state, the MXene textile was repeatedly sprayed with fluorinated carbon nanodot tubes, enabling the modified MWCNTs and carbon black to integrate with the textile. The resulting structure, featuring micro-nano roughness and low surface energy imparted by the fluorinated carbon nanodot-tubes, allowed air entrapment within the rough surface, effectively resisting liquid penetration (Fig. 1d).

The sensing mechanism of SCNCT relies on the deformation between carbon nanomaterials and textile fibers. During deformation, the encapsulating carbon nanomaterials undergo fractures or overlaps at specific locations due to their limited elasticity, causing variations in the material's signal in response to the applied strain (Fig. 1e). The SCNCT surface remained dry even under water jet impacts (Fig. 1f) and submerged conditions (Fig. 1g). As expected, the as-prepared SCNCT, with a CA >160° and SA<5°, exhibits resistance to water, acid, alkali, and saline droplets (Fig. 1h). Therefore, it could realize long-term service in wet and underwater environments (Fig. 1i and j).

The distribution of carbon nanodots, nanotubes, and MXene on textiles is crucial for the SCNCT's overall performance. As shown in Fig. 2 , the structure and morphology of SCNCT was characterized by SEM, EDS, and LSCM. SEM images (Fig. 2a–c) reveal distinct surface features of the hydrophilic textile, MXene textile, and SCNCT at various magnifications. Hydrophilic textile, woven from smooth fibers, features an approximately flat fiber surface (Fig. 2a). Notably, the WCA of its surface was less than 90°. In contrast, MXene textile exhibited a rough, hierarchical structure with MXene sheet uniformly coating the textile fibers (Fig. 2b), achieving a hydrophobic surface (CA 90°–150°). Moreover, SCNCT's micro-nanoscale rough surface, consisting of linear substances and dispersed particles embedded within the lamellae, provides a favorable structural foundation for its superhydrophobic properties (Fig. 2c). EDS element mapping (Fig. 2d) showed a uniform distribution of elements such as C, F, O, N and Ti on SCNCT. This morphology and element distribution suggest uniform grafting of MXene and fluorinated carbon nanodot-tubes onto textiles, laying the foundation for both conductivity and superhydrophobicity. The LCSM images revealed that hydrophilic textile possessed a significant surface roughness (Ra) of 20.04 μm (Fig. 2e). However, SCNCT surface demonstrated a lower Ra of 10.17 μm, featuring continuous stacking of micro/nano-scale structures with densely packed mountain-like protrusions [41] (Fig. 2f).

To elucidate the characteristics and superhydrophobic mechanism of SCNCT, we employed FTIR and XPS to analyze its surface chemical composition. Fig. 3 a demonstrated the presence of characteristic absorption peaks for organic compounds, including CO, C–O, and C–H, within the wavelength range of 1100–1710 cm−1, alongside weak peaks for C–Ti and C–F in the 504–723 cm−1 range [42,43]. This spectral signature is attributed to the uniform dispersion and encapsulation of MXene and fluorinated carbon nanodot tubes around fibers. XPS spectra of MXene-chitosan (Fig. 3b–d) revealed prominent signals for F1s, O1s, Ti2p, and C1s, along with weaker signals for N1s and Cl2p. The presence of C–Ti bond was evident in both C1s and Ti2p spectra, and no impurity elements were detected, attesting to the successful etching of MAX and the integration of chitosan with MXene [44]. The XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 3e) further confirmed the strong signals for F1s, O1s, and C1s, with the high-resolution spectrum (Fig. 3f) specifically showing the presence of C–F bonds. Moreover, we conducted an XRD analysis of hydrophilic fabrics, MXene fabrics, and SCNCT (Fig. S1). The diffraction patterns exhibited peaks at 2θ angles of approximately 11°, 5.7°, and 54°, corresponding to flexible textile [19], MXene [22], and fluoride [34], respectively. These findings collectively demonstrate the successful fluorination of the carbon material, satisfying the elemental prerequisites for achieving superhydrophobicity.

The superhydrophobic robustness of SCNCT is essential for its sustained performance under extreme conditions. To evaluate its mechanical stability, we monitored changes in contact angle (CA) and sliding angle (SA) when subjected to external forces, such as tension, impact, peeling, and abrasion [45]. Fig. 4 a showed that SCNCT maintained robust superhydrophobicity, with a CA greater than 155° and a SA less than 8°, even after 1000 cycles at 50 % strain. CA and SA were tested after every 100 cycles, up to 1000 cycles (same below). The micro- and nano-rough structures remained unaltered (Fig. 4g). Impact resilience, evaluated by dropping a 50 g weight from a 20 cm height, remained satisfactory over 1000 cycles (Fig. 4b). SEM images (Fig. 4h) further verified the structural integrity post-impact. Similarly, after withstanding 200 tape peeling cycles (every 40 cycles), SCNCT maintained its hydrophobic properties (Fig. 4c), and SEM images (Fig. 4j) revealed the enduring rough structure and surface material, indicating its excellent peel resistance. Considering the textile's sensitivity to friction, we assessed SCNCT's friction stability. After abrasion with 1000-grit sandpaper under a 100 g load, the CA remained essentially unchanged, while the SA showed a slight increase (Fig. 4d). SCNCT's surface morphology remained virtually intact post-abrasion (Fig. 4k). Ultimately, SCNCT retains its rough structure even under mechanical damage, due to the strong bonding between MXene and the textile, as well as the interpenetration of fluorinated carbon nanodot tubes within the layers.

Given the challenges posed by complex liquids, UV radiation, and corrosive environments to flexible strain sensors, we proceeded to evaluate SCNCT's chemical stability. When subjected to droplets spanning pH 1–13, SCNCT maintained a CA exceeding 155° and a SA below 6° (Fig. 4e), showcasing its resilience against common corrosive liquids. Even after 60 d of UV irradiation coupled with continuous airflow, SCNCT preserved its superior superhydrophobicity (Fig. 4f), affirming its exceptional chemical durability. To test corrosion resistance under more severe conditions, SCNCT was immersed in 0.1 M HCl, NaOH, and 3.5 wt% NaCl solutions for 3 h, notably retaining superhydrophobic properties (Fig. 4i) and highlighting exceptional corrosion resistance. For comparison, a hydrophilic MXene textile served as a control. Both materials were soaked in 0.1 M acidic and alkaline solutions for 3 h, then dried, and analyzed via SEM for morphological alterations. The hydrophilic MXene textile showed severe corrosion in acidic conditions (Fig. 4l) and even greater damage in alkaline solutions (Fig. 4n). In contrast, SCNCT maintained an unblemished surface, adorned with dense spherical particles, demonstrating excellent superhydrophobicity and corrosion resistance in both environments (Fig. 4m, o). In short, SCNCT demonstrates outstanding mechanical and chemical stability, owing to its multidimensional interlocking structure comprising layered MXene and net-like fluorinated carbon nanodot tubes. This unique structure imparts a robust binding force, ensuring durability for applications in harsh environments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing properties and human monitoring capability
ce:section.ce:para: SCNCT features a continuous and compact conductive network composed of interlocking structures of MXene-based points, lines, and surfaces, endowing it with excellent conductivity, sensing capabilities and robustness. In stretching-release cycles, we monitored the resistance change ratio (ΔR/R0) of SCNCT, which exhibited a low initial resistance of 4.07 kΩ, using Fluke equipment (Fig. 5 a). This enabled us to assess its sensitivity, strain detection range, and long-term stability. Tensile experiments yielded the strain-stress curve, and resistance changes were recorded across a tensile range of 0–100% to establish the relationship between ΔR/R0 and strain (ε). The gauge factor (GF), defined as ΔR/R0/ε and serving as a key indicator of sensitivity [46], revealed distinct sensitivities for SCNCT across various strain ranges, with GF values reaching 10.84 and 32.43. This sensitivity variation in SCNCT arises from its multi-dimensional structure, where an efficient conductive network is formed by MXene and MWCNTs interwoven on textile fibers, further enhanced by carbon black through the tunneling effect. Initial spacing expansion and micro-crack formation enable micro-strain detection (Fig. S2a). With increasing stretching, crack propagation induces a substantial rise in ΔR, enabling response to large deformations (Fig. S2b). At higher strains, substrate stretching exacerbates crack formation (Fig. S2c), reducing conductive pathways and altering resistance, leading to a notable increase in GF.

At a stretching speed of 400 mm/s, SCNCT exhibited a 2 mm extension with a response time of 135 ms and a recovery time of 270 ms (Fig. 5b), highlighting its rapid response sensitivity and recovery capability. The sensing curve remained stable across varying tensile forces and fluctuated consistently with changes in force (Fig. 5c), indicating high sensitivity and a minimum detection stress of 0.9 kPa for small strains and stresses [47]. Within a 15% strain range and across different frequencies, the material demonstrated consistent peak values (Fig. 5d). Furthermore, within the 10–50% strain range and at a constant frequency, the electrical signal curve was stable (Fig. 5e), confirming good responsiveness and repeatability. Additionally, the sensing signal of SCNCT remained stable under various bending angles (Fig. 5f), suggesting its potential for monitoring human activities or mechanical movements at different speeds.

The long-term stability of the SCNCT holds paramount significance for practical applications. After 2000 strain cycles (0–20%) at 480 mm/min, the cyclic curve of SCNCT remained stable (Fig. 5g). Additionally, SCNCT's stability was assessed in vibratory and impact environments. Following a 2 h ultrasonic cleaning test, the sensing curve's peak value and frequency remained unchanged (Fig. 5h), indicating good sensing stability under ultrasonic conditions. In impact test, dropping a 50 g weight from a 20 cm height left the sensing curve's peak value unchanged, but the frequency decreased (Fig. 5i). This suggests potential crack formation in the conductive network and carbon black movement, causing reduced sensing sensitivity and response time, and subsequent frequency discrepancies. SCNCT was subjected to a temperature sensitivity evaluation, involving heating at 70 °C for 2 h and storage at −10 °C for 2 h, respectively. The sensing curve's peak value, frequency, and shape remained unaltered after heating (Fig. 5j). Following freezing, while the frequency increased, the peak value and shape remained unchanged (Fig. 5k), due to the hardening of the conductive network structure, enhancing sensitivity to force changes. Through rigorous testing, SCNCT has exhibited a broad sensing range, high sensitivity, and robust strain responsiveness, maintaining stable performance across both dynamic and static conditions, and demonstrating adaptability to diverse and complex environments.

SCNCT, characterized by flexibility, super-hydrophobicity, durability, high sensitivity, and rapid response, suits monitoring human stretching behaviors for assessing sensing capabilities. We first evaluated and compared the gas permeability of SCNCT (Fig. S3). A small beaker was filled with a high-concentration hydrochloric acid solution, and samples (hydrophilic textile and SCNCT) were secured over the beaker's mouth using rubber bands. A larger beaker, with a pH strip adhered to its inner bottom, was then placed over the small beaker. Timing commenced simultaneously and ceased upon full color change of the pH strip. The results showed that the pH strip on SCNCT changed color in a similar timeframe to that on the hydrophilic textile, confirming SCNCT's excellent gas permeability and further supporting its feasibility for wearable applications on the human body [48]. To facilitate strain monitoring, SCNCT was connected to a Fluke device via copper wires, constructing a sensing equipment capable of interfacing with various body parts. As shown in Fig. 6 a, SCNCT adhered to the throat captured strain from swallowing actions, generating corresponding response signals that confirmed its ability to detect simple swallowing motions. When placed on the chest, SCNCT recorded breathing frequencies during walking and running, revealing similar sensing curve shapes across motion states but with distinct frequencies and peak values (Fig. 6b). These results demonstrate SCNCT's capacity to detect subtle bodily deformations, accurately reflecting respiratory movements under varying conditions [49,50].

To monitor sensing signals from various motions on the same body part, SCNCT was worn around the subject's neck (Fig. 6c). Sensing curves exhibited similar shapes during upward and downward head tilts, with the lowest points corresponding to peaks during downward tilts. The smooth valleys in these curves are due to resistance changes in overlapping conductive networks as the material bent. As bending increased, resistance initially decreased, then increased, and finally decreased again, highlighting the material's sensitivity to changes in surface contact area and internal conductive network alterations. When the subject lowered their head at different angles (30°, 60°), the sensing curves maintained consistent shapes but with varying peaks, confirming the material's accuracy and stability (Fig. 6d). Rightward head tilts resulted in high, sharp peaks in the sensing curve (Fig. 6e), suggesting significant changes in the conductive network, potentially due to the degree of tilt or shifts in the leftward internal network. Conversely, during head turning, the internal trend leaned left, leading to higher peaks during rightward rotations (Fig. 6f).

When worn on the limbs, SCNCT monitors and analyses sensing signals across diverse strain conditions. As shown in Fig. 6g, despite varying peak values at different arm and elbow bending angles, the sensing curves retained consistent shapes, confirming SCNCT's accuracy and stability. Additionally, SCNCT effectively monitored muscle contractions (Fig. 6h). During wrist bending, the sensing curve exhibited a lower peak value compared to arm and elbow movements, owing to the smaller bending angle and more uniform tension at the wrist (Fig. 6i). Finger bending generated sensing curves with consistent peak values but some shape variability, paradoxically underscoring the material's sensing sensitivity (Fig. 6j). When attached to the calf muscles, SCNCT provided responsive monitoring signals during muscle relaxation and tension, enabling personalized exercise adjustments and standardization based on signal fluctuations (Fig. 6k). Moreover, SCNCT monitored knee movement changes (Fig. 6l), revealing sensing curves with varying shapes and peak values across different motion states, with running exhibiting higher peaks and faster frequencies. SCNCT has once again demonstrated exceptional sensing sensitivity and stability in monitoring a wide range of human motions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stable sensing properties under amphibious environments
ce:section.ce:para: Traditional strain sensors frequently suffer from moisture-induced erosion in wet conditions, leading to conductive network short-circuits. Environmental corrosive elements further intensify this degradation, ultimately compromising the sensor's functionality. However, superhydrophobic properties provide a reliable solution, effectively blocking the infiltration of rainwater and soil. This approach offers direct anti-fouling benefits and indirectly maintains the integrity of sensors, extending its operational lifespan. To assess the antifouling capabilities, SCNCT was positioned at a 10° angle in petri dishes and exposed to various solutions including water, acid, alkali, and saline (Figs. S4(a–d) and Movies S1). These liquids formed spherical shapes on the SCNCT surface and quickly rolled off, underscoring SCNCT's exceptional direct antifouling and anticorrosion abilities. Given the presence of soil in natural environments, we assessed the indirect antifouling capabilities of SCNCT (Figs. S4(e–h) and Movies S2). observations indicated that upon liquid contact, a notable amount of dust was efficiently removed, highlighting SCNCT's remarkable indirect antifouling attributes. To evaluate deeper into the antifouling characteristics of various materials, hydrophilic MXene textile and SCNCT were both tested under 20 % soil moisture content and applied weight pressure. Results revealed that soil strongly adhered to the hydrophilic MXene textile, persisting even at angles approaching 90° (Fig. S4i and Movies S3). By contrast, soil on SCNCT effortlessly slid off at inclinations below 60°, leaving no residue (Fig. S4j and Movies S4), exhibiting superior resistance to soil adhesion.

To assess the sensing stability of SCNCT in contaminated environments, we tested the sensing performance of both hydrophilic MXene textiles and SCNCT under conditions involving continuous droplet deposition and dust coverage. Fig. 7 a illustrated that sensing signals of hydrophilic MXene textiles, exhibiting regular under dry conditions, turn chaotic and intensity reduction upon exposure to liquids, especially in acidic and alkaline environments. Conversely, sensing curves of SCNCT remained stable, consistently unaffected by external environmental changes (Fig. 7b). Similarly, when dust-covered hydrophilic MXene textiles were wetted by droplets, significant signal alterations occurred (Fig. 7c). SCNCT still maintained stable sensing signals (Fig. 7d), uninfluenced by dust and droplets. High humidity environments easily induce surface condensation, severely compromising sensors’ performance. For a comparison, hydrophilic MXene textiles and SCNCT both were exposed to water vapor from an air humidifier for 5 min, and a Petri dish collected droplets falling from materials (Fig. 7f). When the humidity at 40–60%, hydrophilic MXene textile became wet, with no droplets in the petri dish (Figs. S5(a and b)), suggesting vapor condensation into the textile. Prior to condensation, hydrophilic MXene textile exhibited regular sensing patterns. Post-condensation, the sensing curves became disordered (Fig. 7e). However, water vapor condenses into droplets on SCNCT surface, and these droplets then fall into the petri dish due to gravity (Figs. S5(c and d)), indicating SCNCT not be wetted. Notably, SCNCT maintained relatively stable sensing frequencies and comparable sensing ranges both before and after condensation (Fig. 7g).

Surface wetting notably undermines material structure and performance by enabling direct contact with water, acids, alkalis, saline solutions, and solid contaminants (Fig. 7h–k). LSCM images revealed that hydrophilic MXene textiles, when wetted by corrosive liquids and slurries, showed fiber surfaces enveloped and eroded by contaminants (Fig. 7i–l). Comparison of pre- and post-wetting images and surface roughness of the hydrophilic textile (Figs. S6a and b) indicates a loss of surface structure, replaced by infiltrating contaminants (Fig. S6c). These corrosive contaminants severely disrupted the conductive network, causing short circuits and sensing signal disruptions (Fig. 7j–m). SCNCT's uninterrupted operation in polluted environments, along with its maintained performance throughout condensation cycles, highlights its exceptional moisture resistance. This robustness originates from the stable superhydrophobic surface of the multidimensional interlocking carbon material, effectively shielding the internal conductive network and ensuring reliable application in high humidity environments.

The infiltration of water molecules and hydrostatic pressure pose significant challenges for the application of flexible sensors in aquatic environments, due to hardly achieve underwater performance comparable to that in air. To evaluate the underwater sensing proficiency of SCNCT, we compared the sensing signals of hydrophilic MXene textile and SCNCT in air, underwater, and re-exposure to air. Fig. 8 a showed that sensing signal of hydrophilic MXene textile remains stable in dry state, yet it diminishes significantly underwater and presents disorder upon return in air. This behavior originated from enhanced conductivity upon water infiltration, leading to reduced sensing peaks (Fig. 8f). The presence of residual moisture upon return in air contributes to the irregularities seen in the sensing curves. In contrast, SCNCT exhibited stable sensing curves both in air and underwater (Fig. 8b). Although the peaks are lower underwater, the sensing signal reverts to its initial level upon re-exposure to air. The operating mechanism of SCNCT involves the induction of a dense layer of small bubbles upon immersion in water, due to its unique surface microstructure and low surface energy. This bubble layer effectively isolates moisture, safeguarding the internal conductive pathways from interference. As a medium, these tiny bubbles transmit external water pressure to SCNCT, eliciting changes in the sensing signals. Upon re-exposure to air, the water pressure diminishes, promptly restoring the sensing signal to its original state [51] (Fig. 8g). Fig. 8c showed that SCNCT generates corresponding response signals to different bending angles underwater, confirming its ability to monitor underwater human movement changes. Compared to finger bending in air (Fig. 6j), the weaker signals further evidencing SCNCT's sensitivity to stress. To assess the impact of hydrostatic pressure to resistance, SCNCT was gradually soaked from air into water to a depth of 20 cm while monitoring the sensing signals (Fig. 8d). SCNCT's resistance initially dropped sharply with increasing depth (red), followed by a gradual decrease as the depth reaches 20 cm, with a slower decline rate (blue). The initial compression of an increasing area as SCNCT transitions from air to full soaking, causing significant changes in the sensing curve. Once fully soaked, the pressure increases with depth, but the compressed area remains constant, resulting in a gradual change in the sensing curve. As pressure increases, the density of the conductive network rises, minimizing structural changes, and the rate of resistance decrease diminishes with increasing depth until the sensing curve stabilizes. To test the structural stability of SCNCT underwater, signal changes were monitored during 400 cycles of 90° finger bending underwater (Fig. 8e). A comparison of the resistance response curves before and after 5 bending cycles (top panel in Fig. 8e) revealed no significant changes in shape and peak values, demonstrating SCNCT's exceptional dynamic durability underwater.

To simulate the application of SCNCT in marine environments, strain sensing was tested in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. Fig. 8h illustrated that hydrophilic MXene textile presents significant signal change upon re-emergence from saline water compared to fresh water (Fig. 8a). A notable difference in signal variation of SCNCT in saline water versus air (Fig. 8i) is attributed to the combined effects of hydrostatic pressure and ions in saline solution (Fig. 8g). The superhydrophobic surface, separated from the saline by an air layer of small bubbles, facilitated ion transfer to SCNCT, influencing the sensing signal in saline. Upon returning to air, the signal nearly reverted to its original level. Subsequently, sensing sensitivity of SCNCT in the saline solution was evaluated through bending at angles of 15°, 30°, 45°, 60°, and 90° (Fig. 8j). Despite the affected signals, distinct response signals were still generated at different angles. To assess the influence of ions in saline on sensing signal strength, we re-immersed SCNCT from air in saline environment. During SCNCT's descent into the saline, its sensing signal rapidly diminished, with significant variations lost at depths between 6 and 8 cm below the water surface (Fig. 8k). Upon initial immersion, sensing signals of SCNCT marked decrease, influenced by both pressure and ions. Once fully submerged, the pressure's effect weakened and ions continued to influence signal changes independently of depth. Finally, sensing signals of consistent periodic variation in saline be monitored (Fig. 8l), highlighting SCNCT's potential for seawater applications.

In summary, a superhydrophobic carbon nanodot-tube/MXene/microfiber coupled textile was developed by constructing an interwoven conductive and rough structure on an elastomeric polyurethane textile, utilizing flake-like MXene, linear MWCNTs, and carbon black particles. SCNCT exhibits superhydrophobicity after strong mechanical damage (1000 tensile strains, 1000 heavy object impacts, 2 m length friction and wear, and 200 tape peels) and being immersed in a strong acid and alkali solution of 0.1 M for 3 h. Moreover, SCNCT presents good sensing sensitivity (GF = 10.84, 32.43) and response time (125 ms), with stable sensing signals under complex conditions, including 2000 tensile fatigue, 2 h of ultrasonic oscillation, 100 heavy object impacts, 2 h of heating at 70 °C, and 2 h of storage at −10 °C. SCNCT can monitor human tensile strain behavior of the neck, throat, breathing, arms, elbows, fingers, and knees. Importantly, SCNCT exhibits excellent sensing performance in wet and underwater environments due to the multi-dimensional interlocking structure. SCNCT achieves accurate strain monitoring underwater and in saline, presenting stable sensing signals after 400 sensing cycles of underwater and in saline. This work proposed simple design and practical means for the development of superhydrophobic flexible strain sensors under amphibious conditions.

Elastane textile was selected as the substrate. MAX (Ti3AlC2, 200 mesh, 99.8 %) powder was purchased from Forsman. Concentrated hydrochloric acid (HCl, 36.0–38.0 %, 12 M) was supplied by China National Pharmaceutical Group Corporation. Lithium fluoride (LiF, 99.9 %) was provided by Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd. Hydroxyl-functionalized multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs-OH) and carbon black (>98 %) were obtained from Jiangsu Xianfeng Nano-Materials Technology Co., Ltd. 1H,1H,1H,2H,2H-Perfluorodecyltrimethoxysilane (FAS) was acquired from Tianjin Damu Chemical Reagent Factory. Glacial acetic acid was purchased from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory. Chitosan (>85 %) was supplied by Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. All other chemicals were commercially available and used as received without further purification.

First, LiF (5 g) was dissolved in 100 mL of HCl (9 M), stir for 1 h, and then gradually add 5 g Ti3AlC2 powder. Second, stir the mixture at 35 °C for 24 h to etch the Al layer. Centrifuge and wash the obtained product to remove impurities. Subsequently, multiple layers of Ti3C2Tx nanosheets were harvested by centrifugation and diluted to a 10 mg/mL MXene suspension. In additional, dissolve glacial acetic acid (2 mL) in distilled water (100 mL), stir and heat for 15 min to obtain a 2 % acetic acid solution. Then add 1 g of chitosan and stir until completely dissolved to obtain a 10 mg/mL chitosan solution. Finally, MXene suspension and chitosan solution were mixed in a ratio of 10:1 to prepare MXene-chitosan composite suspension.

Anhydrous ethanol (100 mL) was transferred into a beaker, to which MWCNTs (0.6 g) and (0.2 g) carbon black were added. After stirring, 0.3 g fluosilane was added dropwise, followed by magnetic stirring at 45 °C for 2 h to ensure complete reaction of the components. Subsequently, the solution was subjected to ultrasonic oscillation to enhance the dispersion of the solutes, resulting in the preparation of a superhydrophobic conductive liquid. A 50 × 15 × 1 mm3 spandex textile was cut, cleaned with anhydrous ethanol, and dried for use as a substrate. The textile was soaked in MXene-chitosan suspension for 5 min and dried at 60 °C, repeating the process to achieve a semi-dry, conductive state. The textile was then coated with superhydrophobic conductive liquid, dried semi-dry in between applications, and fully dried to form a film. Wires were attached to create SCNCT.

The droplet contact angle and sliding angle on the sample were measured by optical contact angle meter (JCY-2, Fangrui) at room temperature. Its mean value was obtained from measurements at four different locations on the same sample. Microstructural images of the material were obtained by scanning electron microscope (SEM, Apreo2C, ThermoFisher, USA), energydispersive spectrometry (EDS), and laser scanning confocal microscopy (OLS4100, LSCM). The surface chemistry of the material was characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray optoelectronic energy spectrum (XPS, Thermo Fisher, USA), and X-ray diffraction (XRD). The general test machine (XM-DZSC001, Yitong Testing Equipment Technology Co., Ltd.) and Fluke2638A were combined to record the loading strain, and synchronize the real-time remote monitoring of the resistance.

Haoyang Song: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Yibo Liang: Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Junchi Ma: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Yang Cai: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision. Yibo Wang: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision. Ximan Wang: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision. Hongjia Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision. Changsheng Liu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition. Yongquan Qing: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Funding acquisition.
All data supporting the findings of this study are available within the article and the Supplementary Information file.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic ResearchFoundation (2024A1515240037), and the Liaoning Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (2023-MS-079).
